author: @Ken00001010
text: Edison was also very good at reworking his failures into something useable. He loved electromagnets and got this idea to use those to concentrate  iron containing particles in ore so he could mill cheap iron ore into something economically valuable. He built an entire town to do that with electromagnets the size of skyscrapers, between which he dropped crushed ore, in an attempt to catch iron rich particles deflected by the field as they fell. The process did work, but his competitors also owned the railroads so they just increased his shipping costs to take out his advantage. So, it was a good idea because it technically worked, but a bad idea because it solved the wrong economic problem. However, in order to make it work, Edison had to develop a new technology to crush ore in vast quantities, which happened to be useful (good idea) in making cement, so he turned around an investment disaster and made plenty of money off cement. I think there is an important lesson, here. Edison could have easily started off to make better cement, but that would not have occurred to him as a "good idea" because it was not technically an area that excited him. Anyone experienced in inventing technology and starting companies will tell you that no business plan survives first contact with the real marketplace. You will not know your good ideas from your bad ideas until you have been there, and done that.
